data.meaning_mnemonic:
This radical means <radical>orders</radical>, because it looks like Napoleon with his big bicorne hat, giving orders. You move, or his orders move you.

See how his body looks like the stamp radical (卩)? If it helps, you can think of Napoleon's written orders as bearing his stamp of approval. All official orders need the stamp of that guy under the big hat, you know?

This radical is the same as the kanji you learned earlier, but the meaning is a little different. It's not just for saying things humbly, it's for being <radical>humble</radical> in general. When you see this radical, think about being humble.

You can kinda see this as someone with their hands pressed together in front of their body, in a very humble posture. Maybe a humble Buddhist monk wearing some big robes? Imagine him all humble there, head bowed, hands pressed together.

This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>older brother</radical>.

In case you need a refresher, here's the mnemonic again: Who's someone who's just a mouth with legs? That's your know-it-all older brother, who won't shut up about anything.

Remember the stairs (乃) radical? This is a lot like that, but it has another stroke to motorize it. The extra bit makes the stairs move by themselves — that's why this is an <radical>escalator</radical>!

This is a ceremony radical with a slide radical. Why would a ceremony need a slide? For <radical>drunkard</radical>s, of course! People love to drink at ceremonies, and when they've had too much, those drunkards like to go down slides. If your ceremony has a slide, the drunkards will be happy.

This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>skin</radical>.

Here's a mnemonic refresher just in case: You go down a slide made of branches and your skin scrapes right off — ouch! Lucky for you, it was just your old outer layer of skin, and now you've got a fresh shiny layer. Yay for exfoliation! You're not sure what to do with this big, floppy layer of old skin, though…

Imagine a <radical>root</radical> vegetable, like a turnip or something. See the twisted bulb at the top, with long, stringy root tendrils dangling under it? That's why this is the root radical.

Alternatively, focus on the components: sun and kick. When the sun is out, you're outside kicking things around. But be careful — kick a root, and you might trip over it!

This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>sound</radical>. You can even see someone standing at a kind of square table here. It's a DJ at the turntables! Imagine them spinning records and scratching out all kinds of cool sounds.

You could also remember this with the kanji mnemonic we gave you. Here's the gist of it: If you stand on the sun, you'll hear a very loud sizzling sound.

This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>few</radical>.

Here's a mnemonic refresher, just in case: You have a small slide. The thing about small slides is that <kanji>few</kanji> people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down. That's why this means "few"!

This the radical for <radical>simple</radical>. Why? Because it looks like a simple little stick figure drawing! Just a big head on top with a few hairs, and a stick figure body. What could be more simple?

In <radical>prison</radical>, <radical>Wolverine</radical> gets picked on for the <radical>barb</radical>s coming out of his hands. This creates a lot of <kanji>conflict</kanji> between him and the other prisoners. They don't take kindly to mutants, and Wolverine doesn't take kindly to bigots — hence the conflict.
data.meaning_hint: Picture one Wolverine versus, like, a hundred prisoners. Who would win? Yeah, my money's on the mutant in this conflict.
data.reading_mnemonic: This constant <kanji>conflict</kanji> between Wolverine and the other inmates is really affecting his <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Despite what people think, Wolverine really doesn't like to fight. He just hates injustice so much, he feels he has to do something about it.
data.reading_hint: Put yourself in Wolverine's shoes. Imagine what it would feel like to have conflicting values, what it would do to your soul. Wanting to be peaceful on the one hand, but on the other not wanting to allow bigotry of any kind. It's a conflict in your soul.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> who takes the <radical>middle</radical> of the road on every issue is likely to have an average <kanji>relationship</kanji> with everyone. He doesn't really take sides in matters, and just stays in the middle. This makes him seem like a wishy-washy people-pleaser, and as such he's only able to maintain average, mid-tier relationships.
data.meaning_hint: I'm sure you've heard of flip-flopping in politics, when people try to take both sides of an issue, staying in the middle ground and never taking a stand. Imagine one of these people to represent this kanji.
data.reading_mnemonic: Imagine a flip-flopping politician you have an average <kanji>relationship</kanji> with. Now imagine that there's a <reading>knocker</reading> (なか) on their forehead and you get to knock that knocker as hard as you like. Maybe you can knock some integrity into them and form a better relationship.
data.reading_hint: Also, this kanji is pronounced exactly like the vocab word 中 (なか). If you can remember that, it'll make remembering 仲 easier.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>cloud</radical> that he uses to <kanji>transmit</kanji> all of the messages he needs to send to his people. The cloud floats above him and when he wants to tell people something he just whispers into the cloud and it transmits his message really, really loudly to all of them.
data.meaning_hint: Picture this special cloud floating above your leader. It's probably just a speaker attached to a drone covered in cotton balls.
data.reading_mnemonic: As the leader <kanji>transmit</kanji>s his latest series of messages to you and the others, you notice a pretty large <reading>den</reading>t (でん) in it. That dent is probably a clue to what this cloud is really made of.
data.reading_hint: You highly doubt this is a magical cloud that can transmit messages. You inch closer to the big dent and try to poke it. Your finger goes right in! Hmm, you're still pretty sure there's a trick to this thing.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The card game fish are best at is <radical>blackjack</radical> because they can easily hold the cards with their <radical>fins</radical> <kanji>together</kanji>. In blackjack you don't have to have a huge hand, full of cards. It's just a few, so fish fins can keep them together and just hit or stay.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a fish trying to hold a full hand of cards together. Impossible! Fish don’t have fingers to keep them spread properly. Now imagine a fish trying to hold two cards together and nodding to hit. It's much easier to keep things together this way!
data.reading_mnemonic: Once a year trillions of fish get <kanji>together</kanji> in <reading>きょう</reading>と to play blackjack. They have a huge tournament there once a year.
data.reading_hint: Imagine a bunch of fish gathering together in きょうと to participate in this blackjack tournament. With so many fish all together in one place, it probably smells pretty fishy.

data.meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>woman</radical> and a <radical>child</radical> in front of you. You <kanji>like</kanji> them very much.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a woman and a child you like very much. Just appreciate them, and think about how much you like those two. The reading mnemonic definitely won't ruin your image of them or anything.
data.reading_mnemonic: "I <kanji>like</kanji> you two," you say to the woman and child. They both turn their heads 180 degrees back towards you. They both have faces that look just like <reading>こう</reading>いち's. "We like you too," they say in unison.
data.reading_hint: And you DO like them. You have always been a fan of こういち's kanji learning methods, and you feel so lucky to have married a woman who looks just like こういち and then (this is the real miracle!) gave birth to a child that looks like こういち too. Hashtag blessed.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.
data.meaning_hint: Picture the drunkard standing in front of you. He picked up the sword and is holding it above his head. It's becoming... becoming...
data.reading_mnemonic: The sword has <kanji>become</kanji> a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! It's just like the saber you have! Hey, wait.
data.reading_hint:
If a sword becomes a saber in the hands of a drunkard... then what are you? Geez this became pretty deep.

You slice down the drunkard with your saber and become a little more depressed than you were before.

data.meaning_mnemonic: By the time you finish digging a <radical>coffin</radical> hole with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>, you’ll be an old <kanji>elderly</kanji> person. Digging with nothing but a spoon takes a long time, especially when the hole needs to be big enough for a coffin. You might as well be digging it for yourself at this rate. By the time you finish, you’ll be all wrinkled and elderly and just about ready to keel over.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself digging a coffin hole with nothing but a spoon. Your hands get all cramped, your wrist starts to hurt, and you get painful blisters on your hands. It takes forever to dig a hole big enough for a coffin!
data.reading_mnemonic: Now that you’re <kanji>elderly</kanji> (and probably about to die) you suddenly crave some delicious <reading>roe</reading> (ろう). Salmon roe, sea urchin roe, tofugu roe, doesn’t matter. Your old, tired bones need roe after all that hard work!
data.reading_hint: You hated roe when you were still young, but now that you’re an elderly old fart you really want to eat it. Roe is for old people, you guess, as you slurp it up.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> <radical>stand</radical>s at the highest <kanji>rank</kanji> in an organization. The leader is of the highest rank and stands above all others.
data.meaning_hint: Picture this great leader. Imagine him actually standing on the shoulders of his supporters who are a lower rank than he is. He’s standing atop this great pyramid as a fearless leader.
data.reading_mnemonic: This great leader is actually the leader of the boy scouts. And as we all know, the highest <kanji>rank</kanji> in boy scouts is the rank of <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) scout. This great leader has attained the rank of eagle scout and stands above all other scouts.
data.reading_hint: Picture the same ranking pyramid from before, only all the members are boy scouts, and the leader at the top is an eagle. Hear the piercing screech of the eagle with your ears.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of your <radical>clan</radical> wants to live close to the <radical>ground</radical> so he demands you get <kanji>low</kanji>. Low, low, with your belly to the ground. That way you're always below him, since he's the leader.
data.meaning_hint: Picture yourself, face smooshed into the ground, and you can hear the leader shouting above you, "GET LOW!"
data.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Low</kanji> people like you can only eat low food like potatoes. But that's boring, so you low people learned to make <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots. You roll them to each other and eat them without lifting your heads.
data.reading_hint: Get low to the ground right now and ask your mom or dad to roll some tater tots towards your face. Eat them up, mm mm.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You wear a special <radical>outfit</radical> and equip a <radical>sword</radical> whenever you do something for the <kanji>first</kanji> time. Doing something for the first time is always scary, but the right outfit and a sword at your side can give you the confidence and courage to overcome your jitters!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine the butterflies in your stomach as you prepare to do something for the first time. Now look down at your badass outfit and the sword on your belt. Feel the nervousness subside a little. You've got this.
data.reading_mnemonic: So, what is it you're doing for the <kanji>first</kanji> time? A fashion <reading>show</reading> (しょ). Your special outfit and sword aren't just to calm your nerves — they're also what you'll be showing off on the catwalk.
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself strutting down the runway at this fashion show. You're doing pretty well for your first show — until you trip over your sword and fall flat on your face. Darn it — now everyone's gonna know this was your first time.

data.meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> you put a <radical>sword</radical> and a <radical>knife</radical>. Now that you have them in there, you have to make them <kanji>separate</kanji> from each other.
data.meaning_hint: Anyone can put a sword and knife in their mouth. But not everyone can make them separate while still in your mouth.
data.reading_mnemonic: Why are you trying to make a sword and knife <kanji>separate</kanji> in your mouth? Because someone <reading>bets</reading> (べつ) you can't do it.
data.reading_hint: "I bet you can't separate the sword and knife," they say in an annoying voice. Alright, time for you to show them. Nobody bets you anything without you blindly trying to do it!

data.meaning_mnemonic: Cut <radical>grain</radical>s with a <radical>knife</radical> to make them smaller and you'll make a great <kanji>profit</kanji>. By cutting them, now you can get three grains worth of money for what was once a single grain.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine buying some rice. Now cut all the grains with a knife into smaller grains. Now sell them for two, three, four times the profit!
data.reading_mnemonic: To make an even better <kanji>profit</kanji>, you decide to cut up <reading>ree</reading>ds (り) too. You stuff the grains in the reeds and sell that as "self-cooking" sets.
data.reading_hint: Picture all of your reeds full of grains in a row. The entire world will benefit from your crafty invention. And you'll turn a great profit too, which certainly doesn't hurt.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> sits on a <radical>stool</radical> to conserve her <radical>power</radical>, as her servants <kanji>toil</kanji> away in the kitchen. They toil away preparing a feast for this powerful woman while she kicks back on her cozy stool. She's a queen and deserves to be pampered.
data.meaning_hint:
Imagine you're one of this woman's minions, toiling away in the kitchen. The heat from the stoves is intense, and you're hoping she doesn't see the sweat dripping from your forehead into the food. Man, you've never toiled so hard in your life.

Note: There are a few other kanji with similar meanings. This one is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on making a concerted effort.
data.reading_mnemonic: As a servant you <kanji>toil</kanji> not just for the woman, but also for her <reading>do</reading>g (ど). Anything she gets, the dog gets too. That means double the toil for you. But hey, at least the dog is cute.
data.reading_hint: Secretly it's this dog you toil for, not the woman. Try to picture what kind of dog it is. And imagine sneaking extra portions to it under the table when the woman isn't looking. Yes, if it weren't for this innocent dog, you'd have done with this life of toil!

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> in a position of <radical>power</radical> does not need to <kanji>labor</kanji> in the fields. Labor is for the weak! A Viking of power takes what he wants, and need not labor for it. A powerful Viking plunders all and takes what he wants!
data.meaning_hint:
Imagine yourself as a powerful Viking. You have the power to take anything you want, pillaging and plundering to your heart’s content. Imagine all the things you would acquire with this sort of power.

Note - there are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words related to physical, or manual labor, with an emphasis on how darn tiring it is!
data.reading_mnemonic: It takes a lot of <kanji>labor</kanji> to harvest <reading>roe</reading> (ろう) so that’s what the Viking decides to take first! Roe is delicious, but it’s small and delicate. Vikings don’t labor for their own roe! They take it and eat it!
data.reading_hint: Picture this powerful Viking laying siege on a small fishing village and taking all the roe and stuffing it into his bearded face. So much labor went into harvesting that roe! Vikings are so mean!

data.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Orders</radical> coming from your <radical>mouth</radical> will determine the <kanji>fate</kanji> of your soldiers. You are their commanding officer and your orders determine their <kanji>life</kanji> and death.
data.meaning_hint: Pretend you are a high ranking officer and imagine yourself giving orders to your underlings. Imagine all the power and influence you have. It feels good.
data.reading_mnemonic: You are the commanding officer on the <reading>May</reading>flower (めい). You give orders to colonize America. The orders spewing from your mouth hole determine the <kanji>fate</kanji> of the settlers and the fate of America and the fate of the world! You and the Mayflower have changed everything forever.
data.reading_hint: Students of American history are very familiar with the Mayflower. Recall learning about it in school whenever you see this kanji. Or look it up on Wikipedia if you don’t know about it. The Mayflower is a big deal!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> on top of the <radical>cliff</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> from the <kanji>coast</kanji> below. Just from looking at the raging <kanji>shore</kanji> below, you'd think everything would be wet, it's splashing everywhere!
data.meaning_hint: You're on top of the mountain and the entire coast is stretching out below. The shore goes as far as your eyes can see.
data.reading_mnemonic: Sitting up there, watching the <kanji>coast</kanji> with you, is <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). The world famous Gandhi, is just hanging out up here with you, looking down at what you now realize must be a coast in India.
data.reading_hint: Sit down next to Gandhi and close your eyes. You can hear the water hitting the shore below. The coastal birds are screaming. You crack open an eye and see Gandhi still sitting perfectly still.

data.meaning_mnemonic: As the year starts moving in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical>, the trees must <kanji>release</kanji> their leaves.
data.meaning_hint: Picture winter heading straight for you and frosting everything over. Now picture the leaves being released by the trees.
data.reading_mnemonic: The trees <kanji>release</kanji> all of their leaves onto your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Slowly, you watch in horror as your home is consumed by the released leaves. They enter in through the windows, doors, and the chimney.
data.reading_hint: "Release my home from this leaf prison!" you shout at the now bare trees.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You've been playing <radical>blackjack</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical> since <kanji>long ago</kanji>. This is the longest game of blackjack you've ever played and it feels like ages since you started. You've been sitting out in the sun playing blackjack since long, long ago.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself playing a never-ending game of blackjack out in the hot sun. You’re sticky, sweaty, and tired. But all you can do is continue to play your game of blackjack. It’s miserable.
data.reading_mnemonic: Once you finally finish the game, you walk the long walk back home, in the sun, barefoot, on a dirt road that hasn't been maintained since <kanji>long ago</kanji>. Your feet are all sweaty from being out in the sun, and walking on this dirt road gives you <reading>muck ashi</reading> (むかし). Your ashi (feet) are all mucky and muddy from the combination of dirt and sweat. Your muck ashi are really gross, man.
data.reading_hint: Imagine your muck ashi, all dirty and muddy from walking on that old road from long ago. Feel the muck between the toes of your ashi, squishing with every step. Hear the sound the mud makes as it slides between your sweaty mucky toes.

data.meaning_mnemonic: What part of a <radical>tsunami</radical> do you feel with your <radical>skin</radical>? The <kanji>wave</kanji>s. Tsunamis are accompanied by all kinds of terrifying sights and sounds, but what you feel on your skin are the powerful waves.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine being struck by the waves of a tsunami. Feel the force of their impact and the overall sensations on your skin. Those are some mighty waves.
data.reading_mnemonic: When each <kanji>wave</kanji> hits your body, you shout, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!" It's not like it's on purpose. When the wave crashes into you, the wind gets knocked right out of you, making you say "Hah!"
data.reading_hint: The waves are basically punching you in the gut, forcing the air out of your lungs, and making you shout "Hah!"

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>master</radical> has the power to <kanji>pour</kanji> water wherever he pleases. Tsunami masters are masters over the most powerful water force in the universe, so it makes sense that they have the ability to pour water at will, wherever they please. Best not tick off the master, unless you want a tsunami poured on your head.
data.meaning_hint: Picture yourself as this tsunami master. You’re like a water bender from Avatar, only more awesome. Feel the cold wet water pouring out of your fingertips as you create water out of nothing. Imagine what it would be like to have this power.
data.reading_mnemonic: If <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) was trying to attack you, you’d be able to defend yourself easily. Simply <kanji>pour</kanji> a tsunami on him and Chewbacca would surrender in a heartbeat. Chewbacca doesn’t like having water poured on him, especially not all the water from a tsunami.
data.reading_hint: Imagine Chewbacca soaking wet. All his fur is matted down and he looks pretty depressed. He smells like wet dog too. Smell this smell in your nostrils as you imagine how pathetic this drenched Chewbacca feels.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Give <radical>trash</radical> to the <radical>moon</radical> and it will <kanji>nurture</kanji> and <kanji>raise</kanji> that trash like it's its own little moon baby.
data.meaning_hint: Picture the moon raising this ball of trash, giving it love and attention.
data.reading_mnemonic: The moon <kanji>nurture</kanji>s the trash ball until one day, it speaks! "<reading>行く</reading> (いく), 行く！" it says. It wants to go live with mom moon!
data.reading_hint: Watch as the little trash ball 行くs up into the sky and floats around its moon mama. If you raise your child right it will want to 行く wherever you go too.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> always seem to <radical>suit</radical> the random things you <kanji>pick up</kanji> off the ground. An old thimble. A discarded onion ring. A dead worm you tie in a knot. Your incredible fingers suit them beautifully, almost as if they were jewelry, so of course you pick up all of them.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine finding something that suits your life and your style. You're not just going to leave it there, right? No, you're going to pick it up! Go on, use your fingers and pick up that thing.
data.reading_mnemonic: You're walking around looking for something to <kanji>pick up</kanji> when you see a sword in a rock. Yes, this will suit you. You pick up the sword and people jump out around you and tell you that you are the new <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ)! Uh oh. Maybe you shouldn't have picked this up.
data.reading_hint: You picked up the hero sword and now you're the new hero. Time to go kill a dragon. Good luck, and try not to die, hero person!

data.meaning_mnemonic: Try to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to <radical>spoon</radical> out some of the <radical>sun</radical> and you'll come back with just one <kanji>finger</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Look down at your finger and the burned stumps where the rest of your fingers used to be. It hurts. And you're sad. But at least you still have one finger left.
data.reading_mnemonic: Don't worry, you aren't the only one out there with just one <kanji>finger</kanji>, <reading>shee</reading>p (し) only have one finger too. I mean, they're hooves, which are really just a single animal finger. It's just big and kind of flat.
data.reading_hint: Picture sitting with a sheep. It's right across from you, staring into your eyes. It reaches out its sheep finger hoof and you reach out with your finger. They touch. It's magical.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
This <radical>tsunami</radical> is ridden by a <radical>sheep</radical> in classic <kanji>Western style</kanji>. And by Western style I mean the sheep is dressed in a cowboy outfit and has put a saddle atop the tsunami wave to ride it. It's a tsunami rodeo and this crazy sheep is riding that tsunami wave in classic Western style.

This kanji also means <kanji>ocean</kanji>, because presumably the people from the West, with their Western style, came from across the ocean. You'll want to be familiar with both meanings for vocab later on.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine how ridiculous a sheep would look all decked out in Western-style cowboy gear. Now imagine it riding a tsunami wave as it screams, "Baa~ baa~ baa~!"
data.reading_mnemonic: After a whole day of riding this tsunami, the sheep is hankerin' for some <kanji>Western-style</kanji> food, namely <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Yogurt is Western-style food, right? If it helps you can imagine the yogurt is flavored like apple pie, to make it more American and Western.
data.reading_hint: Imagine yourself eating this Western-style apple pie yogurt. It tastes just like apple pie, only creamier and fruitier. Taste the sweet yogurt in your mouth as you imagine yourself scarfing some down.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>spirit</radical> should feel <radical>humble</radical> before a <kanji>god</kanji>'s <kanji>spirit</kanji>. You never really feel humble in front of anyone else, but if you meet a god's spirit… Well, you should probably lower yourself appropriately. Gods have powerful spirits.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a spirit appearing before you. It's a god. You should be as humble as possible. Out of respect and also because you don't want to be cursed or something.
data.reading_mnemonic: When you meet a <kanji>god</kanji>'s <kanji>spirit</kanji>, you need to get down on your <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Get down on your ankles? No, not low enough. Knees? No, no, get your shins flat on the ground to show how humble you really are. Luckily, you're wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん), so at least that will stop your poor shins getting chafed!
data.reading_hint: If you don't get down on your shins before god's spirit, it could take them away. Even your jeans couldn't protect you then. You don't want to live a shin-less life, do you?

data.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Grain</radical>s are <radical>few</radical>. Imagine the last few grains trickling down the hourglass. You only have a <kanji>second</kanji> left.
data.meaning_hint: Normally an hourglass has sand to count the seconds, but yours uses grains. Imagine just a few grains left, and watch them trickle down as you count a second, and then another second.
data.reading_mnemonic: After the final <kanji>second</kanji>s go by, your awful <reading>B.O.</reading> (びょう) appears.
data.reading_hint: Your hourglass timer was a timer to specifically let you know when your B.O. was set to return.

data.meaning_mnemonic: There are pieces of <radical>thread</radical> on each <radical>escalator</radical> showing you what <kanji>level</kanji> they go to in your school. Each escalator takes you to a different <kanji>grade</kanji>, so make sure you follow the right thread or you'll end up in a class that's either way above or way below your level.
data.meaning_hint: When you level up your Japanese skills, you'll take this escalator to rank up too!
data.reading_mnemonic: You want to see what the highest <kanji>level</kanji> is, so you go up the wrong escalator. At the top you find a giant <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう).
data.reading_hint: A teacher comes over and says, "Excuse me, this is advanced level cucumber studies. I don't think you're at this level yet... please take the escalator down and follow the threads to find your level."

data.meaning_mnemonic: On a <radical>scooter</radical> there is a <radical>bear</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on its head trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> and <kanji>chase</kanji> you! You accidentally splashed it with water and it's not happy about it. You're on your own scooter, luckily, but that doesn't make this any less terrifying.
data.meaning_hint: Picture the bear on its scooter with the offending drop of water on its head following really closely behind your scooter. You can almost feel his bear breath on your neck!
data.reading_mnemonic: The bear is trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> you because it wants your <reading>o</reading>bi (お) as compensation for its injured pride. The obi is waving in the wind behind your scooter, and the bear is reaching out with its teeth to try and grab it.
data.reading_hint: Picture the bear chasing you on its scooter, trying to reach out and grab the obi off your waist. The drop of water on its head is jiggling from the exertion. Woah, it's getting a little too close!

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>simple</radical> <radical>drunkard</radical> starting a <kanji>war</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine what the simple drunkard does, to start the war. They're not in their right mind, and the centaurs (getting you ready for the reading mnemonic!) are an easily offended people. Go ahead and run through the simple drunkard's actions toward the centaurs that start this war.
data.reading_mnemonic: The simple drunkard started a <kanji>war</kanji> with the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん)! This is especially unfortunate, because the centaurs are really good at <kanji>battle</kanji>s because they're all automatically cavalry troops.
data.reading_hint: Imagine the war: humans vs centaurs. Tens of thousands clashing in battle.

data.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical>. Now <radical>stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical> again. You wish to stand on, and above, your older brother. You <kanji>compete</kanji> over and over again, to stand atop your older brother.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself standing on your older brother. They push you off. So you do it again. You want to be on top, but your older brother doesn't want you to. You compete for the top spot over and over again, throughout your whole life, because that's what one does with their older brother.
data.reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>compete</kanji> with your older brother because only one of you may go to <reading>きょう</reading>と. Whoever stands on top the most gets to go to きょうと, so you must compete for a spot.
data.reading_hint: Imagine yourself competing to stand on top of your older brother. Do you win? Do you get the one ticket to きょうと?

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>drop</radical> of water to a <radical>root</radical> is <kanji>good</kanji>. It's good for the roots to grow and live and thrive. And what's good for roots is good for our plants and us humans too.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine putting a little drop of water over a root you want to grow. Doing this is good for the plant and also good for your soul.
data.reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>good</kanji> way to get a lot of drops of water for your roots is to buy a <reading>row</reading> boat (りょう). Then just row out to the middle of the lake and plonk all the roots right into the source!
data.reading_hint: Rowing even feels good on your arms and back. Probably just as good as all these roots will feel.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Using your <radical>construction</radical> <radical>power</radical> you build something great. A true <kanji>achievement</kanji> built for the achievement of making kanji learning easier!
data.meaning_hint: Just constructing this thing was an achievement. Feel proud like it was, well, an achievement.
data.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>achievement</kanji> was made possible by <reading>こう</reading>いち, so of course this construction project is a giant statue of the man himself. What an achievement of a statue. What an achievement of an achievement.
data.reading_hint:
Thanks こういち. Now everyone will know of your achievements around kanji learning and inside-out earth theory.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading. Remember that, because it comes up quite a bit!

data.meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>cow</radical> <radical>temple</radical> is <kanji>special</kanji>. This cow must have been very important to have an entire temple dedicated to it.
data.meaning_hint: Picture a temple dedicated to a single cow. All of the people who come and worship here have a special place in their hearts for the cow.
data.reading_mnemonic: This cow is <kanji>special</kanji> because it belonged to <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu, the first shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate and uniter of Japan! He's a super important figure in Japanese history. You should absolutely look him up and read a bit about him (when you're caught up on these lessons, that is).
data.reading_hint: You walk up to a statue of the special cow in the temple and see a collar around its neck that says "Tokugawa Moo Moo" on it. How... special!

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> stands upon the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. She is searching for <radical>treasure</radical>. It is said that this treasure here in this rice field will make things much easier for the leader (that's what leaders really want, after all). If she gets this treasure, it will be a great <kanji>convenience</kanji> for her, because convenience is everything when you're the leader.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine the leader looking around the rice paddy. She keeps saying, "Ugh, it would be convenient if I didn't have to walk around out here looking for the treasure that's going to make things more convenient for me. Ugh." Go ahead and say this phrase out loud, too, pretending to be the leader in the rice paddy. Feel the cold and the wet, making it less convenient for you.
data.reading_mnemonic: She finds a place to dig in the ground. It looks like there's some fresh dirt here. Finally, the treasure of <kanji>convenience</kanji> that will make this leader's life so much easier. She digs into the ground and pulls out a box. When she opens it, she finds out it's a <reading>ben</reading>to (べん). "My god," she thinks. "This really will make my life more convenient. My people can take their lunches with them now instead of coming back to base to eat, which will waste way less time!"
data.reading_hint: Feel the happiness that the leader would feel at this revelation. It will make everything much more convenient for her, since she's not the one doing all the manual labor. She won't have to eat out of a bento, that's for sure.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> makes you lift <radical>heavy</radical> loads with all your <radical>power</radical>, since this is what you do for <kanji>work</kanji>. Why do you do this kind of work? Well, you're good at it and the leader makes you do it, I guess.
data.meaning_hint:
Picture this leader asking you (yet again) to work for them. "Can you move those heavy objects with all that power of yours?" Typical leader — as soon as they know you're good at a certain kind of work, they ask you to do it all the time. 

Note: There are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on movement or operating something.
data.reading_mnemonic: Where do you get the power to do all this <kanji>work</kanji>? From <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう). You eat nothing but doughnuts. If you had no doughnuts, you couldn't work. In fact, your leader pays you for your work… in doughnuts.
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself receiving a doughnut as payment after you've been working. It's delicious. Doughnuts are fantastic. Taste the fresh doughnut in your mouth as it crumbles and melts on your tongue. It's the best doughnut you've ever had.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If you know the radical meaning <radical>orders</radical> you know the kanji meaning <kanji>orders</kanji> as well!
data.meaning_hint: Here's an order: study your radicals.
data.reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>orders</kanji> are to get a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun.
data.reading_hint:
Imagine an authority figure giving you this order as an official order. Ray guns don't really exist, so you think it's a pretty strange order.

Hint: when you see the <radical>orders</radical> radical, the reading will often be れい. The mnemonic will likely involve orders to get a ray gun of some kind.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>sound</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> gives me an <kanji>idea</kanji>. The methodic thump thump thumping of a heart puts me in a trance that gives me great ideas. It’s a very inspirational sound, the sound of a heart is. It gives me an idea. 

Alternatively, you could look at this kanji and see a "stand" radical and something that kind of (almost) looks like the kanji for "think." When you stand up and think, you come up with your very own <kanji>idea</kanji>s!
data.meaning_hint: Put your hand over your heart, or on your wrist, or somewhere you can feel your pulse. Your heart is pumping blood through your body, hear the sound of your heart beat and try to picture and get an idea for how all the blood flows through your body.
data.reading_mnemonic: The heart of an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) is very strong. The person who knew this probably came up with the <kanji>idea</kanji> to make the bald eagle a symbol of the United States. Eagles are always flying around and doing things, so their hearts are very strong.
data.reading_hint: Picture the majestic animal that is the bald eagle. Imagine the sound of an eagle screeching as it zooms through the air, flapping its wings in the wind. It’s pretty impressive.

data.meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> there is a <radical>jet</radical> dropping <kanji>flavor</kanji> bombs. Every time you <kanji>taste</kanji> something, it's actually because a tiny jet has flown into your mouth to bomb you with flavor.
data.meaning_hint: What's your favorite food? Imagine that, as you bite into it, tiny jets are released from inside the food to attack your tongue with flavor bombs, stimulating your sense of taste.
data.reading_mnemonic: And what has more <kanji>flavor</kanji> than a big slab of <reading>me</reading>at (み)? You love meat and its flavor nearly as much as you love the <kanji>taste</kanji> of its juice. So many good things to put in your mouth!
data.reading_hint: Of course, if you're vegetarian imagine the flavor of the meat being terrible. Up to you. Make the mnemonic work for your lifestyle.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Who will <radical>say</radical> <radical>turkey</radical>? Who? <kanji>WHO</kanji>???
data.meaning_hint: Say "who" over and over again, demanding someone says "turkey," for some reason.
data.reading_mnemonic: Then, someone says turkey. "<kanji>Who</kanji> said turkey? You? How <reading>dare</reading> (だれ) you."
data.reading_hint: You're so mad at that person even though you demanded someone say turkey.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <kanji>sell</kanji>ing that you've raised (上げ) is the <vocabulary>amount sold</vocabulary> or number of <vocabulary>sales</vocabulary>. Think of a graph going upward, where it's rising as you sell more things.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading for 売 is the one you learned with 売る. The reading for 上げ is the same as the reading for 上げる, a word you learned previously. If you know those two separate things, you should be able to combine them into one awesome vocab thing.

context_sentences.en: The sales have gradually declined.
context_sentences.ja: だんだん売り上げが落ちてきた。

context_sentences.en: How's the DVD selling?
context_sentences.ja: DVDの売り上げはどうですか？

context_sentences.en: Sales have decreased rapidly because of that sexist advertisement.
context_sentences.ja: あの性差別の広告のせいで、売り上げが激減した。

data.meaning_mnemonic: When the <kanji>sell</kanji>ing is <kanji>cut</kanji>, that means something is <vocabulary>sold out</vocabulary>. You'll see this word a lot in stores (or, hopefully you won't...) in Japan. Look out for it if you're able!
data.reading_mnemonic: This word uses the kun'yomi readings for both kanji, but you know them both now, so you should be able to read this too!

context_sentences.en: Ramen is all sold out for today.
context_sentences.ja: 今日のラーメンは全部売り切れです。

context_sentences.en: The popular band's concert tickets were sold out in a minute from the release.
context_sentences.ja: 人気のバンドのコンサートチケットは、発売から一分で売り切れてしまった。

context_sentences.en: This book was sold out for a long time, but it finally came back in stock!
context_sentences.ja: この本、ずっと売り切れだったんだけど、やっと再入荷したのよ！

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how 手 can mean “doer”? Here, we have a <kanji>sell</kanji> <kanji>doer</kanji>. Who might that be? It’s someone who does selling – a <vocabulary>seller</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>vendor</vocabulary>.

売り手 is usually used when talking about sellers versus buyers.
data.reading_mnemonic: This word uses the kun'yomi readings for both kanji, but you know them both now, so you should be able to read this too! One hint that this is a kun'yomi reading word is the hiragana in the center. It's like two separate words combined together, and when a word is a single kanji / a single kanji with hiragana attached it is usually kun'yomi. This is like two of those, combined.

context_sentences.en: The seller was a man.
context_sentences.ja: 売り手は男でした。

context_sentences.en: The seller of the house was a famous idol singer.
context_sentences.ja: 家の売り手は有名なアイドルでした。

context_sentences.en: Isn't this transaction too advantageous for the seller?
context_sentences.ja: この取引、売り手にとって有利すぎない？

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <kanji>now</kanji> <kanji>night</kanji> is the current night, aka <vocabulary>tonight</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You haven't learned the on'yomi reading for 夜 yet, so here's a mnemonic:

<vocabulary>Tonight</vocabulary>, you're going to a <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や) baseball game. Not just any game, not just any night — the Yankees, tonight.

context_sentences.en: The wedding party is tonight.
context_sentences.ja: ウェディングパーティーは今夜です。

context_sentences.en: I will go to the gym alone tonight.
context_sentences.ja: 今夜は一人でジムに行きます。

context_sentences.en: You may see Mercury in the west tonight.
context_sentences.ja: 今夜は西の方に水星が見えるかもしれません。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The <kanji>East</kanji> <kanji>North</kanji> area of Japan is called <vocabulary>Tohoku</vocabulary>, or the <vocabulary>Tohoku region</vocabulary>. It was the region hit by the 2011 Tohoku Earthquake. 

This can also mean <vocabulary>northeast region</vocabulary> when talking about China or Japan, but for other countries (and for the compass direction), we'd use 北東.
data.reading_mnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You haven't seen the on'yomi reading for 北 (ほく) yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

In <vocabulary>Tohoku</vocabulary> there's a lot of <reading>hocu</reading>s (ほく) pocus going on. Basically, if you're in Japan's northeast, watch out… A lot of mountain hermits live up in those parts, practicing their weird hocus pocus. Don't let 'em getcha.

context_sentences.en: I was volunteering in Tohoku.
context_sentences.ja: 東北で、ボランティアをしていました。

context_sentences.en: Tohoku beef is delicious.
context_sentences.ja: 東北の牛肉はおいしいです。

context_sentences.en: My grandmother is originally from Tohoku, so she understands the dialect.
context_sentences.ja: 私のおばあちゃんは東北生まれなので方言が分かります。

data.meaning_mnemonic: When you <kanji>decide</kanji> with your <kanji>heart</kanji> you have true <vocabulary>determination</vocabulary>, unlike when you decide with your mind.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Just make sure you take note of the shortened つ. So, けつ goes to けっ.

context_sentences.en: I determined to propose on Mt. Fuji.
context_sentences.ja: ふじ山の上でプロポーズすると決心した。

context_sentences.en: My determination to "become a hero" is strong.
context_sentences.ja: 私の「ヒーローになる」という決心は強いです。

context_sentences.en: Have you found the resolve to study chemistry at university?
context_sentences.ja: 大学で化学を学ぶ決心はつきましたか？

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You <kanji>enter</kanji> a <kanji>facility</kanji>. So this is your <vocabulary>admission</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>entrance</vocabulary> into that facility. 

入所 is for entering facilities or institutions, often those with names ending in 所, like a 研究所 (research institute). It's also used in prison contexts. You might gain "admission" to prison for not doing your WaniKani reviews, for example, at least in countries ruled by Crabigators...
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: My grandma joined the group home in March.
context_sentences.ja: 私のおばあちゃんは、三月にグループホームに入所しました。

context_sentences.en: I had an entrance test for a model agency today.
context_sentences.ja: 今日はモデルエージェンシーの入所テストでした。

context_sentences.en: Be sure to check the rules before your admission.
context_sentences.ja: 入所の前に必ずルールをチェックしておいて下さい。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how 始める means "to begin something"? 始め is its noun version and means "beginning," as in the start of an action or process. に marks a point in time. So 始めに means <vocabulary>in the beginning</vocabulary>. 

始めに can also mean <vocabulary>to begin with</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to start with</vocabulary> when introducing an opening action, like 始めに、ウォーミングアップをしましょう (let's begin with some warm-up exercises).
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading is kun'yomi (look at all that hiragana!) but that's the reading you learned with the word 始める, so have no fear, you got it!

context_sentences.en: First, I'd like to make a presentation.
context_sentences.ja: 始めに、プレゼンテーションをしたいと思います。

context_sentences.en: Cut the cabbage first.
context_sentences.ja: 始めにキャベツを切ります。

context_sentences.en: I ate pasta on my first day of work this year.
context_sentences.ja: 今年の仕事始めにはパスタを食べました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The <kanji>real</kanji> <kanji>thing</kanji> is the <vocabulary>real thing</vocabulary>, aka the <vocabulary>real deal</vocabulary>. 

If you learned 本 as <kanji>book</kanji> you can also think of this word as the "book thing." What is this book thing? Is it the real version of the book? Is it the real deal? If so, I'll pay you a million.
data.reading_mnemonic: The readings are on'yomi plus kun'yomi (kun'yomi is 物). You learned the reading of the kanji 本 and also the vocabulary reading of 物, so you should be able to read it.

context_sentences.en: Is this lettuce perhaps real?
context_sentences.ja: このレタス、もしかして本物なの？

context_sentences.en: I bought these Nike sneakers thinking they were real.
context_sentences.ja: このナイキのスニーカー、本物だと思って買いました。

context_sentences.en: Real rich people won't even complain about their duty of paying taxes.
context_sentences.ja: 本物の金持ちは税金を払う義務について悪口なんて言わないよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You know that 乗り has something to do with <kanji>riding</kanji> and that 物 is a <kanji>thing</kanji>. So what is a "riding thing?" Nowadays, at least, that would be a <vocabulary>vehicle</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: This word uses the kun'yomi readings for both kanji, but you know them both now, so you should be able to read this too!

context_sentences.en: I got motion sickness.
context_sentences.ja: 乗り物よいしちゃった。

context_sentences.en: I loved riding things when I was little.
context_sentences.ja: 小さいころ、ボクは乗り物に乗るのが大好きでした。

context_sentences.en: I feel like old vehicles smell like time itself.
context_sentences.ja: 古い乗り物には、時間のにおいがする気がするんだよね。

context_sentences.en: In Japan, it is considered good manners to set your smartphone to silent mode and refrain from making calls on public transportation.
context_sentences.ja: 日本では、公共の乗り物の中ではスマホはマナーモードにして通話はしないのがマナーです。

data.meaning_mnemonic: When you <kanji>hold</kanji> <kanji>gold</kanji> you are a <vocabulary>rich person</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: Do you remember the word お金? This is the 金 in that word. The reading for 持ち is the も you learned with 持つ. So, combine the かね of お金 and the 持ち and you have the reading of this word. Note that just like お金, you'll often see this with the honorific prefix お in front of it as well.

context_sentences.en: There are lots of rich people's houses around here.
context_sentences.ja: この辺は金持ちの家が多い。

context_sentences.en: It's the kind of school where rich people would go to.
context_sentences.ja: そこは金持ちが行くような学校だよ。

context_sentences.en: I used to run small errands for a wealthy neighbor of mine to make money when I was young.
context_sentences.ja: 若い頃、お金を稼ぐために、近所のお金持ちの人たちの使い走りをしていました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
The <kanji>energy</kanji> you hold (持つ) within you is your <vocabulary>feeling</vocabulary>s. What kind of feeling is your energy giving you today?

気持ち can refer to your current mood, to a particular emotion inside you, or to physical feelings and sensations. You hear it in phrases like 気持ちいい (feels good), 気持ちわるい (feels gross), or 気持ちが分かる (I understand how you feel).
data.reading_mnemonic: 気 is on'yomi and you learned that with the kanji. 持ち, though, is kun'yomi, which you learned with the word 持つ. One of those weird exception readings, but the ち on the end of 持ち is a clue that it uses the kun'yomi.

context_sentences.en: I know exactly how you feel.
context_sentences.ja: あなたの気持ちはよく分かります。

context_sentences.en: I've been feeling sick since I woke up in the morning.
context_sentences.ja: 朝起きた時から気持ちわるいんです。

context_sentences.en: Here's a little something for you. This is just a small token of my appreciation. Go ahead, open it.
context_sentences.ja: これ、つまらないものですが。ささやかな感謝の気持ちです。どうぞ、開けてみてください。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You are going to <kanji>release</kanji> and <kanji>send</kanji> something out. Imagine this message going out through the sky to everyone around you. You are going <vocabulary>to broadcast something</vocabulary> to the world! 

The する is on there to make this noun a verb. Without the する it would be the noun "broadcast."
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: This TV drama will be broadcast for the first time in ten years.
context_sentences.ja: このドラマが放送されるのは、十年ぶりです。

context_sentences.en: Are we really broadcasting this clip?
context_sentences.ja: このクリップ、本当に放送するんですか？

context_sentences.en: President Koichi's important announcement will be broadcast on television tonight.
context_sentences.ja: コウイチ社長は今晩テレビで重大な決定を放送することになっている。

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <kanji>language</kanji> about <kanji>thing</kanji>s. This is a confusing one. Just think of it more like someone talking about things, anything, really. Someone who just talks about things is someone who tells stories. So, this word means <vocabulary>tale</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>story</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>legend</vocabulary> (not the Tom Cruise one, though).
data.reading_mnemonic:
Both kanji take the kun'yomi here. For 物, that's もの, which you learned with the vocabulary 物. You learned the kun'yomi for 語 with the verb 語る, but here it becomes がたり because of rendaku, and the り is tucked inside the kanji.

Maybe you've already heard the word ものがたり too. It's in some film and book names. If you haven't, and you find this reading hard to remember, think of a story about an infamous <reading>Gatling</reading> (がたり) gun that wandered the land, mowing everything down in its path. It's a terrible legend, but what if it were true???

context_sentences.en: This tale is very long.
context_sentences.ja: この物語はとても長いですよ。

context_sentences.en: I'll read the Andersen story for you.
context_sentences.ja: アンデルセン物語を読んであげましょう。

context_sentences.en: There are many stories about yokai in various parts of Japan.
context_sentences.ja: 妖怪にまつわる物語は、日本各地にたくさんあります。

data.meaning_mnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>conflict</kanji> so the vocabulary means <vocabulary>to compete</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to argue</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to quarrel</vocabulary>. Your views conflict with someone else's so you're going to compete with them to see who's right.
data.reading_mnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You're starting <vocabulary>to compete</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>to argue</vocabulary> with someone, who is yelling at you: "You <reading>are so</reading> (あらそ) stupid!". You might want to imagine this in an accent that gets you closer to the Japanese pronunciation.

context_sentences.en: Please don't argue about me!
context_sentences.ja: わたしの事で争うのはよして！

context_sentences.en: I am determined not to lose in a game where we compete for the champion belt.
context_sentences.ja: チャンピオンベルトを争うゲームでは負けないって決めたんです。

context_sentences.en: We've never argued, have we?

data.meaning_mnemonic: When you <kanji>return</kanji> the <kanji>doing</kanji>s of another person, you're doing it to <vocabulary>get even</vocabulary> (whether it's a nice thing or a mean thing).
data.reading_mnemonic: The readings are a bit weird, here. 仕 gets its on'yomi reading... you should be okay with that. 返し gets the kun'yomi reading, though, which is the reading you saw with the word 返す in level 9. You can tell this part uses the kun'yomi vocab reading because it has that hiragana attached to it. Just remember to distinguish between the two parts of the word to remember the readings.

context_sentences.en: I find it quite annoying, so I wanna get even.
context_sentences.ja: ムカつくから、なんか仕返ししたいな。

context_sentences.en: Seriously, she is the worst! I'll get even with her someday!
context_sentences.ja: 本当に最低の女！いつか仕返ししてやるわ！

context_sentences.en: To get back at him for eating my pudding, I ate Koichi's favorite yogurt.
context_sentences.ja: プリンを食べられた仕返しに、コウイチの好きなヨーグルトを食べてやった。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You hopefully know the word 返す, which means "to return" (as in, you're doing the direct action of returning something). This word, on the other hand, is the version where something just returns (and you're not doing the action of returning it). Use the る to help you to remember this. る the Kangaroo is returned to you. You don't do any of the returning, it just happens or someone else does it. That's why this word means <vocabulary>to be returned</vocabulary>.

Note this word is often combined with くる (to come), which reinforces the aspect that something is coming back to you.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading is the same as 返す, so as long as you know that you'll be able to apply it here to learn the reading of this word as well.

context_sentences.en: I'll be at the office until an email comes back.
context_sentences.ja: メールが返ってくるまで会社にいます。

context_sentences.en: The ball that I threw came back to me with the wind.
context_sentences.ja: 投げたボールが風で返ってきた。

context_sentences.en: Thinking my money would be returned properly was a huge mistake.
context_sentences.ja: 貸したお金がちゃんと返ってくると思ったら大間違いよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: What's a <kanji>ride</kanji> <kanji>location</kanji>? It's a <vocabulary>boarding area</vocabulary>! This word is used widely to refer to any place where you board transportation, such as a <vocabulary>bus stop</vocabulary>, a <vocabulary>taxi stand</vocabulary>, or a <vocabulary>train platform</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic:
This word uses the kun'yomi readings. You didn't learn the kun'yomi reading for 場 with the kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember it:

At the <vocabulary>boarding area</vocabulary>, you <reading>baa</reading> (ば) at the bus driver to ask if this is the right bus. You're in a country inhabited only by sheep, but luckily you've practiced your Sheepanese well enough to be able to baa for directions at <vocabulary>bus stop</vocabulary>s, <vocabulary>taxi stand</vocabulary>s, and <vocabulary>train platform</vocabulary>s.

context_sentences.en: Where is the gondola terminal?
context_sentences.ja: ゴンドラ乗り場はどこですか？

context_sentences.en: I'm at the taxi stand now.
context_sentences.ja: 今、タクシー乗り場にいます。

context_sentences.en: At the moment, I’m chilling at the bus stop in front of my house looking at cars passing by.
context_sentences.ja: 今、家の前のバス乗り場で通り過ぎる車を見ながらダラダラしているよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You've seen the word 使う hopefully by now. If you have, you know it means "to use." So, the <kanji>way</kanji> to <kanji>use</kanji> something is the <vocabulary>way of using</vocabulary> (aka <vocabulary>how to use</vocabulary>). This will be a word you see when you're looking at directions for how to use something.
data.reading_mnemonic: This uses the kun'yomi readings from the vocab words 使う and 方. So as long as you're okay with those, you can apply that knowledge over here.

context_sentences.en: You should be more careful about how you use language.
context_sentences.ja: もう少し、言葉の使い方に気を付けた方がいいよ。

context_sentences.en: Grandpa always calls because he doesn't know how to use his smartphone or computer.
context_sentences.ja: おじいちゃんはスマホやパソコンの使い方が分からないので、いつも電話をしてきます。

context_sentences.en: How to use Excel? Even my dog knows that.
context_sentences.ja: エクセルの使い方？そんなのうちの犬でも知ってるよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You know 事 means "occurrence," but it can also mean "thing." So when you're <kanji>doing</kanji> <kanji>thing</kanji>s all day, what are you probably doing? A <vocabulary>job</vocabulary>! All jobs really just amount to doing things.

Just like "job" in English, 仕事 can refer to a person's specific <vocabulary>occupation</vocabulary>, to <vocabulary>work</vocabulary> in general, or to a particular task depending on the context.
data.reading_mnemonic: This word uses the on'yomi reading for 仕 and the kun'yomi reading for 事. Please note that 事 uses the reading こと, which you didn't learn with the kanji, but you have come across it once or twice by now, and because of the way rendaku works, it's changed to the voiced ごと.

context_sentences.en: Since I started a new job, it has only been three days.
context_sentences.ja: 新しい仕事を始めてから、まだ三日です。

context_sentences.en: I won't be able to sign off work until six o'clock today, so stay in a nearby cafe until then.
context_sentences.ja: 今日は六時まで仕事が終わらないから、それまで近くのカフェにいてね。

context_sentences.en: The baby's job is to cry.
context_sentences.ja: 赤ちゃんの仕事は泣くことです。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You've hopefully come across 負ける by now, which means "to lose." So, you can figure out this word means "losing dog." Well, calling someone a dog isn't so nice, so you're probably just calling them a <vocabulary>loser</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The readings are kun'yomi, but you should be familiar with 負ける and of course 犬 well enough to learn the reading here.

context_sentences.en: I don't think you're a loser.
context_sentences.ja: 私はあなたのことを負け犬だとは思わないよ。

context_sentences.en: If I am living like this, I'll just continue to be a loser in life.
context_sentences.ja: こんな生活をしてたら、ずっと人生の負け犬だよ。

context_sentences.en: I don't want to be called a loser anymore.
context_sentences.ja: 負け犬と呼ばれるのは、もう嫌なんだよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You've hopefully seen 勝つ by now, which means "to win." This is a nouny version of that. So, what's the noun version of "to win?" It's <vocabulary>a win</vocabulary> or a <vocabulary>victory</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from 勝つ. So as long as you have some understanding of that reading, you should be able to read this one as well.

context_sentences.en: Japan's team won today's game.

context_sentences.en: The key chain is first come, first served. Let’s get a move on!

context_sentences.en: If that ace is on the mound, we don't stand a chance of winning.
context_sentences.ja: あのエースがマウンドに立っていたら、私たちには勝ち目がないよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You've already been introduced to the word 苦しい. That word means painful, or agonizing. So, if you turn that into a verb, you have <vocabulary>to suffer</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to struggle</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from 苦しい, so as long as you're on top of that word, this word should be readable for you as well.

context_sentences.en: There are many people who suffer from long hours at work in Japan.
context_sentences.ja: 日本には長時間労働に苦しむ人が多くいます。

context_sentences.en: Why do innocent people suffer?
context_sentences.ja: なぜ罪のない人が苦しんでいるのか。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
When you <kanji>attach</kanji> your <kanji>energy</kanji> (imagine someone suddenly looking up at something, attaching their energy to it), you have noticed or realized something. That's why this word means <vocabulary>to realize</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to notice</vocabulary>. 

Don't confuse this with 気を付けて which is similar in that it has all the same kanji. It's just used a little different. Try to remember the difference before moving on.
data.reading_mnemonic: The readings come from the 気 kanji (which you know) and the word 付く which you should know by now. Combine these together and you have the ability to read this word. Take note that the 付く part is rendaku'd, though, so it is a tiny bit different.

context_sentences.en: It took me some time to realize that the woman was my own mother.
context_sentences.ja: その女が自分の母だと気付くまでに時間がかかりました。

context_sentences.ja: 気づいたらもう学校に行く時間だった。

context_sentences.en: I can’t believe you gave me your ice cream. I didn’t realize that you were so kind until now.
context_sentences.ja: あんたが私にアイスクリームをくれるなんて、本当に信じられないよ。あんたがそんなに優しいなんて今まで気付かなかったわ！

data.meaning_mnemonic: You know the words 見る and 送る by now, hopefully. Here you're "seeing" the "sending." You're seeing someone off, so this verb is <vocabulary>to see off</vocabulary>. This can also mean <vocabulary>to put off</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to pass on</vocabulary>, since it's as though you're just passively seeing someone (or something) off rather than taking action and going with them.
data.reading_mnemonic: The readings come from the vocab words 見る and 送る. Make sure you know those and you'll know this word's reading as well.

context_sentences.en: Which do you like being better? One who sees someone off, or one who is seen off by someone.

context_sentences.en: He passed up a chance to go abroad.
context_sentences.ja: 彼は海外に行くチャンスを見送った。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You know the single word 時, right? It means <kanji>time</kanji>. This is that twice. So you have one time, and you have another time. They are separate. Things don't happen in every of these times you have. They happen <vocabulary>sometimes</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading is the vocab word 時 twice, with the second one getting rendaku'd.

context_sentences.en: I go to the convenience store from time to time.
context_sentences.ja: そこのコンビニは時々行きます。

context_sentences.en: Sometimes I wonder what Koichi is doing at the moment.
context_sentences.ja: 時々「コウイチは今何してるかな？」って思うんだよね。

context_sentences.en: Sometimes there were problems with my neighbors.
context_sentences.ja: ご近所とのトラブルは時々ありました。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Imagine someone <kanji>accept</kanji>ing something and then <kanji>attach</kanji>ing something. This person is a <vocabulary>receptionist</vocabulary>, because they are accepting the call, writing down the information, and then attaching said information to a sticky note for you to read. You say, "No, I'm not available."
data.reading_mnemonic: For this reading you have to think back to the verb words that get used with this kanji, namely 受ける and 付ける. Then just drop off the るs and put them together. 受 becomes うけ and 付 becomes つけ and you're left with うけつけ. This is what's called a compound word. And now you know it. Nice!

context_sentences.en: Please first go to the receptionist.
context_sentences.ja: まずは受付に行ってください。

context_sentences.en: Even the reception staff at that gym are ripped.

context_sentences.en: Have you already checked in at reception?
context_sentences.ja: 受付の手続きはもうお済みですか？

data.meaning_mnemonic: You've hopefully seen 買う by now and know it means "to buy." So, 買い is going to be similar to that. Add that to <kanji>thing</kanji> and you have a "buying things" sort of word. What does "buying things" describe? It describes <vocabulary>shopping</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The readings are the kun'yomi, which is usually the case when there's hiragana in the middle. You learned the reading for 買い when you learned the kanji, as well as the word 買う, and you learned this reading of 物 in the word 物!

context_sentences.en: I've just done the grocery shopping.
context_sentences.ja: スーパーで買い物をしたばかりです。

context_sentences.en: Let's go shopping at a department store sometime.
context_sentences.ja: たまにはデパートに買い物に行こうよ。

context_sentences.en: We’re going Christmas shopping today! I’m so excited!
context_sentences.ja: 今日はクリスマスの買い物に行くの！すごくワクワクするわ！

data.meaning_mnemonic: A "suffering hand" is a little misleading. Think more in terms of the words 苦い (bitter) and 手 (person... remember how that happens sometimes?). Now think of this word like you're bitter about having to work with someone. Why? Because they are extremely <vocabulary>hard to deal with</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading is that of 苦い (にがい) plus regular old 手 (て). Remember, this is your bitter person, the person who's "hard to deal with." So, it uses the strange にがい reading.

context_sentences.en: I'm not a big fan of celery.

context_sentences.en: The drawback about that idol is that she is not a good singer.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Something that's <kanji>not</kanji> <kanji>good</kanji> is <vocabulary>bad</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>substandard</vocabulary>. This usage is more formal and frequently paired with other kanji compounds. Alternatively, it can refer to someone who is a  <vocabulary>delinquent</vocabulary>, in which case it is often used as a standalone word.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

context_sentences.en: Viet's hair style today kind of looks like a delinquent's, don't you think?

data.meaning_mnemonic:
When you have a "feeling" (気持ち) that is "good" (いい), you are <vocabulary>feeling good</vocabulary>.

気持ちいい is what you say when something really <vocabulary>feels good</vocabulary>, both physically and emotionally. You can say it after stepping into a hot bath, feeling a cool breeze, or getting a massage that hits just right. It's the Japanese version of leaning back and going "ahhh, this feels nice!"
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from the vocab word 気持ち, so if you know that (or the vocab words 気 and 持つ) you should be A-okay on this one. <vocabulary>Feels good</vocabulary>, right?

context_sentences.en: This blanket feels really good.
context_sentences.ja: このブランケット、気持ちいい。

context_sentences.en: Doesn't it feel good to have a clean house?
context_sentences.ja: 家がきれいだと気持ちいいよね！

context_sentences.en: It looks like I felt so comfortable that I was snoring during the massage.
context_sentences.ja: マッサージ中、気持ち良すぎてイビキをかいてたみたい。

data.meaning_mnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>attempt</kanji> or <kanji>try</kanji>. The verb vocab version is <vocabulary>to try something out</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to give something a try</vocabulary>. Why don't you try out the meaning of this verb, while you're at it?
data.reading_mnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: Okay. Here's the deal. We have this wild animal. I want you <vocabulary>to attempt</vocabulary> to <reading>tame</reading> (ため) it. Now, imagine trying to tame this wild animal. Yeehaw!

context_sentences.en: Try this supplement.
context_sentences.ja: このサプリ、試して。

context_sentences.en: I tried various diets, but none of them worked.
context_sentences.ja: 色々なダイエットを試したけど、どれもダメだったよ。

context_sentences.en: I don’t know if I can lick my elbow, but I’ll give it a try.
context_sentences.ja: 肘を舐めれるかどうかは分からないけど、試しにやってみるよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>compare</kanji> so the verb vocab version is <vocabulary>to compare</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: Look at this <reading>cooler</reading> (くら). Now look at this other cooler. We need <vocabulary>to  compare</vocabulary> the two coolers. Think about all the features each one has and really compare, like you have a list or something.

context_sentences.ja: この二つの写真を比べると、整形したのがハッキリ分かるよ。

data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. It'll also help if you know the two separate words that make up this longer word.

data.meaning_mnemonic: It is <kanji>correct</kanji> you have <kanji>solve</kanji>d the answer, getting the <vocabulary>correct answer</vocabulary>. You'll see this on Japanese game shows a lot. 正解！！！
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You know that the kanji 付 means <kanji>attach</kanji> and words like 付く mean "to be attached." This has the same idea. When you see this attached to another word, for example, you might know that something is attached to something else, or perhaps <vocabulary>included</vocabulary> with it. For example, if you stay at a hotel room, maybe it is a breakfast付き, i.e. "<vocabulary>including</vocabulary> breakfast."
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from the reading of the kanji as well as from words like 付く and 付ける.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You like the <kanji>flavor</kanji> of a particular <kanji>direction</kanji>, so you go that way. Everyone else going in the same flavor direction is your <vocabulary>ally</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>supporter</vocabulary>. They're your flavor direction allies. They have the same tastes and they're chasing the same flavors in life, so naturally they become allies, right?
data.reading_mnemonic:
This word uses the on'yomi reading for 味, but the kun'yomi reading for 方, so watch out for that! You're probably more familiar with the on'yomi for 方, so here's a mnemonic to help with the kun'yomi:

A good <vocabulary>ally</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>supporter</vocabulary> is always ready to <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた) you into the best positions. They believe in you so they want what's best for you.

context_sentences.ja: お父さんは、お姉ちゃんの味方のフリをするが、いつでも私の味方だ。

context_sentences.ja: お前は一体どっちの味方なんだ？

data.meaning_mnemonic: You have 発売, which means "for sale," and then you have 中 which is <kanji>middle</kanji>. data.meaning_mnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means lack, and this verb means to lack something. Pretty simple in English, but 欠く is actually kind of advanced and formal in Japanese, so it's mostly just used in writing. Also keep in mind this is a transitive verb, so 欠く takes a direct object — maybe someone lacks knowledge, for example, or you lack concentration. This is a bit different from saying "your concentration is lacking," at least grammatically. (For that, you'd use 欠ける.)
data.reading_mnemonic:
